w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: is an all-in-one project management platform built to connect daily team tasks with high-level business goals. It moves away from scattered tools by bringing project tracking, team attendance, and performance evaluations into a single, professional workspace
This document describes, in simple business language, what the

web application does. It explains the features available to each type of user and shows how each screen looks in the live system. The document is meant to be read by business stakeholders, project managers, developers and reviewers without needing technical background.
Who need to manage global data, upload bulk records, and oversee every project from a bird's-eye view.
Who need to evaluate team performance and track health metrics across multiple projects.
Who need a functional, Jira-style dashboard to manage their daily stories, tasks, and defects.
is organized around three user roles: Administrator, Project Manager and Developer. Every user

in with their email and password. Based on their role, the system takes them to the right home page and shows only the screens that are relevant to them. The sections below describe each area of the application in plain language.
Login is the entry point of the application. Users enter their email and password to access the system. If a user forgets their password, they can reset it from the same screen.
A signed-in user is taken to the dashboard that matches their role. Wrong credentials show a clear error message. Each user only sees the menus and actions that match their role.
Enter email and password to sign in.
See an error message if the email or password is wrong.
Get redirected to the right dashboard based on the role (Admin, PM or Developer).
A self-service way for users to reset their own password without involving the administrator.
Open the Forgot Password option from the login screen.
Set a new password for the registered email.
Sign in immediately with the new password.

he Administrator owns the workspace. The administrator sets up the projects, the project managers, the team members and the teams. Once the basics are in place, project managers and developers can start working without further setup.
The home page for the administrator. It shows the overall picture of the workspace
and provides shortcuts to every management area.
See the total number of projects, project managers, team members and teams.
View quick health indicators across the organization.
Open any management area (projects, managers, members, teams) from one place.
A central list of every project in the organization. The administrator creates new projects, edits existing ones, or removes projects that are no longer needed.
View all projects with their key details (name, owner, status).
Edit an existing project (rename, change owner, update details).
Delete projects that are no longer required.
A directory of all project managers. The administrator decides who can act as a PM and on which projects.
See the full list of project managers and their contact details.
Add a new project manager to the system.
Edit a project manager’s profile or activate/deactivate them.
Assign or change the projects a PM is responsible for.
Teams group people together to deliver a project. The administrator creates teams and decides who is in them.
View all teams in the organization.
Create a new team and add members to it.
Edit a team (rename, add/remove members, change the lead).
Disband a team that is no longer needed.

From the Admin Dashboard, the administrator can register a new team member. Two options are provided on a single dialog: a bulk upload of an Excel (.xlsx) file for onboarding many people at once, or a manual single-record form to register one person.
Bulk upload a .xlsx file containing many team members and register them in one click.
Manually register one team member by filling in Employee ID, Phone Number, First Name, Last Name, Email and Password.
Close the dialog without saving when needed.
From the Admin Dashboard, the administrator can register a new Project Manager through a focused form. The new manager becomes available immediately for project assignment.
Register a Project Manager with Employee ID, Phone Number, First Name, Last Name, Email and Password.
Close the dialog at any time without saving.
The administrator can create a new project from the Admin Dashboard. The form captures the project basics and lets the admin optionally assign a Project Manager at creation time.
Provide Project Name, Description, Status (default IN_PROGRESS) and Domain (e.g. TECHNOLOGY).
Optionally pick a Project Manager from the dropdown (or leave it as "— None —").
Click Create Project to save, or Close to cancel.

In the Projects Workspace view, an admin sees every project as a card with its ID, name, domain, manager and team. On hovering a project card a "Move to Workspace" button appears. Clicking it opens that project in the workspace, where the admin gets the same set of features available to a developer (Board, Backlog, Timeline, Defects, Test Cases, History, Analytics, Documents and Team Info) so the admin can inspect or assist on any project end-to-end.
Search and filter projects (All / Active / Completed) and view totals (Total, Ongoing, Completed).
Hover any project card and click "Move to Workspace" to open the project workspace.
Inside the workspace, use the same developer features (board, defects, test cases, etc.) for that project.
Figure 2.2.8: Projects Workspace (Admin) with Move to Workspace

Clicking on any project from the Projects list opens the Edit Project Details screen. The administrator can update every field of a project, including reassigning the Project Manager, and can also delete the project from this screen.
Edit Project Name, Description, Status and Domain.
Reassign the Project Manager from a dropdown of all PMs.
Delete the project using the Delete Project button.
Save Changes or Cancel to discard.

Clicking on any project manager from the Project Managers list opens the Edit Project Manager Details screen. The administrator can update the manager's profile and activate or deactivate the account.
Edit First Name, Last Name, Email and Phone Number.
Set Status to Active or Inactive.
Figure 2.2.10: Edit Project Manager Details (Admin view)

The Project Manager plans the work, assigns the right team to the right project, and tracks how the project is progressing. The PM area provides a focused workspace with the views needed to run projects and report on quality and delivery.
The home page for a project manager. It summarizes the projects and teams they own and highlights what needs their attention today.
See the projects assigned to the PM
Track high-level health: active projects, teams and pending items.
Open any project or team directly from the dashboard.
The list of projects the PM is running. From here, the PM manages scope, status and details of each project.
View all projects owned by the PM with status and key information.
Open a project to view or edit its details.
Update project status (active, on hold, completed, etc.).
Capture or refresh project description, dates and other key fields.
it should show move to workspace button and clicking it should redirect to the project workspace.
The teams the PM works with. The PM uses this view to confirm the team line-up and update it when people are added or moved.
See every team linked to the PM’s projects.
Open a team to view its members and the project it supports.
Adjust team composition when needed (add or remove members).
From the PM Dashboard, the project manager creates a new project under their own name. A team can be attached only after the project exists.
Provide Project Name, Description, Status and Domain.
The project is automatically created under the PM who is logged in.
Click Create Project to save or

After a project is created, the PM can create a team for it from the PM Dashboard. The Project dropdown only lists projects the PM manages that do not yet have a team assigned, so a project can have at most one team.
Enter Team Name and pick a project from the PM's own projects without a team.
See helper text confirming members and a scrum master can be added on the next screen.
Click Create Team to save or
and after clicking create team it should redirect to edit team to add members and scrum master

Clicking on a team from the Teams list opens the team edit screen. The PM can rename the team, manage its members, and pick a scrum master from inside the team. Members already in another team are shown as locked so a developer is in only one team at a time.
See the project the team is attached to (read-only chip).
Search developers by name, email or ID and add available ones; remove members with the close icon.
Lock indicator shows which team blocks an unavailable developer (e.g. "In 'Falcon Force'").
Pick a Scrum Master from the selected members (optional).
Clicking on a project from My Projects opens the Edit Project Details screen for that project. The PM can edit only the projects they manage; admins can edit any project.
Edit Project Name, Description, Status and Domain.
See which team is currently assigned to the project (with member count).
Open the Edit Team page directly using the Edit Team button.
Delete the project using Delete Project (only for the PM's own projects).

The Developer does the day-to-day delivery work. The developer area provides a single workspace where developers can see what to do next, track progress, raise defects, manage tests, share documents and collaborate with the team.
The home page for a developer. It surfaces the work assigned to them and provides shortcuts to everything they need during the day.
View personal progress and pending actions.
Jump directly into the board, defects, test cases or documents.
A visual board that shows work items by their status (such as To Do, In Progress, Done). It is the primary working view for the team.
See all work items as cards arranged by status.
Move a card from one status to another as work progresses.
Open a card to view details, comments and attachments.
Filter cards by team member, type or priority.
A list of all the work that has been planned but not yet started. The team picks items from the backlog into the active iteration.
See all upcoming work items in priority order.
Add new work items to the backlog.
Edit a backlog item (description, priority, estimate).
Move items into the current sprint or iteration.
A timeline view of the project. It shows when each piece of work is planned and how items overlap.
See start and end dates for each work item.
Understand how the project is tracking against the plan.
A place to log and track issues found in the product. Each defect captures what is wrong, how serious it is and who is fixing it.
Raise a new defect with description, severity and priority.
Assign a defect to a team member.
Update a defect’s status (open, in progress, fixed, closed).
Search and filter defects by status, severity or owner.
The catalogue of test cases for the project. Each test case describes a scenario that needs to be verified.
Create new test cases with steps and expected results.
Edit or delete existing test cases.
Mark a test case as passed, failed or blocked after execution.
Search and filter test cases by module or status.
A record of activity in the project so the team can see what changed, when and by whom.
See a chronological log of changes to work items.
Track who made a change and when.
Use the history for audit and for resolving disputes.
Charts that summarize the team’s and the developer’s own progress.
See personal and team progress over time.
View completion rates by work item type.
Identify trends that affect the team’s velocity.
A shared space for project documents – designs, specifications, meeting notes and references.
Upload a new document to the project.
Replace or remove a document when it becomes outdated.
Information about the team the developer is part of – members, roles and contact details.
See the full list of team members.
View each member’s role and contact information.
Understand the structure of the team
From the Board, a developer can create a new issue (User Story, Task or Bug). The dialog shows which project and reporter the issue will be filed under.
Pick Issue Type (e.g. User Story) and Status (e.g. To Do).
Enter a Summary and a longer Description.
Set Assignee, Story Points, Sprint and an optional Parent Epic.
Click Create Issue to add it to the
From the Board, a developer can report a defect. The defect is linked to a specific ticket and test case so traceability is preserved end-to-end.
Select the related Ticket and Test Case.
Set Severity (e.g. MEDIUM) and Reproducibility (e.g. ALWAYS).
Add numbered Steps to Reproduce and click Add Step to extend the list.
Click Report Defect to save or
Clicking a card on the Board opens the issue detail dialog. The developer can update the issue inline and follow its activity.
Edit Status, Type, Assignee, Story Points, Sprint and Parent Epic.
Read Comments and History tabs and add a new comment.
From the Defect Tracker, opening a bug shows its full record with traceability, expected vs actual, and lifecycle actions.
See Assignee, Severity, Reproducibility and current Status.
Compare Expected vs Actual results side by side.
Read the numbered Steps to Reproduce.
Switch tabs to Linked Test Cases and Activity.
Move the bug forward with Retest, Mark Fixed or Start Progress.
From the Test Case Management screen, clicking a user story tile opens its detail dialog with key metadata for traceability.
See Story ID, Type, Status and Story Points.
Use this view to confirm coverage before designing test cases.
From Test Case Management, the developer creates a new test case and links it to one or more user stories so the traceability matrix stays accurate.
Enter a Description of what the test verifies.
Pick Test Type (Functional, Negative, UI, Positive, …) and Complexity (Simple, Medium, Complex).
Optionally provide Test Data and define the Expected Result.
Link the test case to one or more user stories.
Click Create Test Case to save or Cancel.
